sentence: Alright, I think we're all on — Raj, Brian, can you both hear me okay?
Yeah, I'm here. Audio's good.
Yep, I'm on. Give me one sec, I'm just pulling up the incident doc.

Sure, take your time. So, uh, the reason I wanted to get us together today — obviously we're still in the middle of this, day three of the outage window — I want to make sure we have a clear remediation plan and that we're all aligned before we send anything else externally.
Yeah, agreed. I've been heads-down with the engineering team since Saturday and I think we have a pretty solid picture of the root cause at this point. Do you want me to just walk through it?
Please, yeah. Go ahead.

Okay so — and Brian, let me know if the technical depth here is useful for you or if you want me to translate as I go — basically what we're looking at is a cascading failure in the event processing pipeline. The short version is we had a single point of failure in the event ingestion layer, and when that node went down, there was no fallback. So events just started backing up and then the whole downstream monitoring stack lost visibility.
Yeah no that's — that's helpful context actually. My team has been getting hammered with tickets since Saturday morning and I want to make sure I understand what I'm telling customers, so.
How many tickets are we at now, roughly?

As of this morning we're at a hundred and twelve open cases specifically tied to the Detect outage. About thirty of those are priority one or two accounts. We've got a handful of customers that are, uh, pretty vocal about it.
Okay. That's — yeah, that tracks with what I've been seeing from the account side. Raj, continuing on the root cause — what's the fix look like?

So the fix is two-pronged. The immediate thing we're doing — and this is already in progress, the team started on it yesterday — is deploying redundant processing nodes. So instead of relying on a single ingestion point, we're moving to an active-active configuration. That should eliminate the single point of failure entirely.
And the second piece?

The second piece is implementing a circuit breaker pattern on the pipeline. So essentially, if one component starts degrading, the circuit breaker trips before it can take down the whole chain. It's a more resilient architecture overall. We should have had this from the start, honestly, but — yeah.

I appreciate you saying that. I think — and I want to be candid here — I think there's going to be a postmortem conversation about how this got built the way it did, but I want to table that for now and focus on the remediation. What's the timeline looking like for full restoration?

Current estimate is we'll have the redundant nodes live by end of day today. The circuit breaker implementation is a little more involved — that's probably forty-eight hours from now, so, Wednesday evening at the earliest. But the monitoring visibility issue, the core thing customers are experiencing, that should be resolved today.
Okay so when you say resolved today — are we talking full visibility restored across all tenants, or is it phased?
It'll be phased. We're going to roll it out starting with, uh, our larger enterprise tenants first and then work down. Realistically all tenants should have visibility restored by tomorrow morning.
Okay. That's a little frustrating because I've been telling customers we're targeting end of today. Can we — is there any way to compress that timeline or is tomorrow morning pretty firm?
Brian, I get it, but I'd rather give customers a conservative timeline and hit it than promise end of day and miss. We've already got a credibility issue here.
Yeah, no, that's fair. That's fair. I just — I need to go back and update a lot of those tickets and some of these customers are going to push back.

I can help draft the updated customer communication if that's useful, Brian. I think we should have something that acknowledges the delay, explains the phased rollout without getting too deep in the weeds technically, and gives them the concrete timeline.
Yeah, that would actually be really helpful. My team is stretched pretty thin right now.
Raj, on the six hours of no visibility — I want to make sure I understand this correctly. During that window, customers had zero threat monitoring data? Like nothing was coming through?

Correct. Events were being ingested at the edge but they were just... queuing up and not processing. So from a customer-facing dashboard perspective, it looked like nothing was happening. No alerts, no detections, nothing. Which is — yeah, that's bad.
And we're confident that there were no actual threats during that window that went undetected? Or is that — can we even say that?

That's a really important question and the honest answer is we've been doing retroactive processing of the queued events and so far we haven't identified any threats that were active during that window, but I want to be careful about saying definitively that nothing happened. We're still working through the backlog.

Okay so we cannot make any affirmative statements to customers about security posture during that window. Got it. Brian, make sure your team is not — if anyone's asking that question, the answer is we're doing a full retroactive analysis and we'll communicate findings.
Understood. Yeah, I'll make sure that's clear in the team briefing. We've had a few customers ask exactly that and I've been kind of hedging, so it's good to have a cleaner answer.
Raj, what's the ETA on completing that retroactive processing? I want to know when we can close the loop on that.

We're probably looking at another twenty-four hours to fully process and review the backlog. I'll have a summary report by Wednesday that we can share internally, and then we can figure out what we communicate to customers from there.

Alright. Let's talk about the postmortem briefly — not to get into the full discussion, but I want to align on the process. Are we thinking internal postmortem first and then a customer-facing summary, or are we doing those in parallel?

I'd want to do the internal one first. We need to have our story straight before we put anything external out. I'm thinking we do a full engineering postmortem by end of this week, and then next week we can work on a customer-facing incident report.
That timeline works for me. Brian, from a support perspective, is there anything you need for the postmortem process? Like data from the ticket queue or customer impact analysis?

Yeah, I can pull together a breakdown of affected accounts, priority tiers, how long each was impacted — that kind of thing. That should be useful for the postmortem. I'll have that ready by Thursday.

Perfect. Okay, so just to summarize where we are — redundant nodes going live today, circuit breaker implementation by Wednesday, full tenant visibility restored by tomorrow morning, retroactive event analysis complete by Wednesday, internal postmortem end of this week, customer-facing incident report next week. Does that capture it?
That's accurate from my end.
Yeah, that matches what I've got. I'll start updating tickets this afternoon once Megan sends over draft language.
Great. I'll get that to you within the hour, Brian. Raj — anything else you need from me or from the product side to unblock the engineering work?
Not right now. The team knows what they're doing. I'll send you both a status update this evening once the first phase of the rollout is done.
Sounds good. Thanks both. Let's talk again Wednesday to review where we are on the circuit breaker and the retroactive analysis. I'll send a hold.
Works for me.
Yeah, talk Wednesday. Thanks everyone.
Thank you for calling Aegis Cloud Security support, this is Sarah Chen, how can I help you today?
Hi Sarah, yeah this is Gregory Fisk, I'm the IT Director over at Summit Trust, I have a support case open — case number 9279 — regarding a billing question.
Great, let me pull that up right now, Gregory — and thanks for the case number, that helps a lot.
Sure, no problem.
Yeah that's fine, just ping me, we can do a quick thirty minutes.
Okay, I'm seeing case 9279 here, Summit Trust, billing inquiry submitted... looks like February 4th. So can you walk me through what you're seeing on your end?

Yeah so, uh, we received our January invoice last week and there's a line item on there that doesn't quite match what we expected based on our contract. It's showing a charge for — let me pull this up — okay, it's showing a charge for 1,400 seats under Aegis Identity, but we only have 1,200 licensed users.
Okay, so a discrepancy of 200 seats on the Aegis Identity module. Got it. And just to confirm — was this your January invoice, so the billing period would have been January 1st through January 31st?
That's correct, yeah, January billing period.
Alright. And do you have the invoice number handy, or should I just work from the account?
Um, give me just a second... yeah, it's INV-2026-00847.

Perfect, INV-2026-00847, let me bring that up... okay, I can see the invoice. And yeah, I can see the Identity module line item — 1,400 seats at the per-seat rate. Let me cross-reference this against your contract terms and your provisioned seat count.
Right, and I mean, our contract was renewed in October, I believe, and at that time we explicitly set the seat count at 1,200. So I'm not sure where the 1,400 is coming from.

Yeah, that's a fair question. Let me look at the contract record... okay so I'm seeing the renewal executed October 14th, 2025, and the contracted seat count for Aegis Identity is listed as 1,200 seats. So you're correct there.
Right, so where's the 1,400 coming from? Is there some kind of overage charge? Because we weren't notified of any overage.

That's actually a good point and let me dig into the usage data for January. Our billing system will sometimes pick up peak concurrent provisioned users during the billing cycle, and if that number exceeded your contracted seat count at any point, it can trigger overage billing depending on how your contract is structured.
Hmm. I wasn't aware that was how it worked. We haven't had 1,400 active users — I mean our headcount isn't even close to that.

Understood, and that's worth looking at more closely. So I'm pulling up the provisioning logs for your account for January... okay so what I'm seeing here is interesting — there were 1,400 user accounts in a provisioned state within the Aegis Identity system during January, but let me look at whether those were all active or if some were in, like, a pending or deprovisioned queue.

Yeah, we did do a migration in December where we were onboarding users from a legacy system, and I know there were some test accounts created during that process. Could those still be showing as provisioned?

That could very well explain it. If test accounts or staging accounts were created and not explicitly deprovisioned, the system would count those against your seat total. Do you know approximately how many test accounts were created during that migration?
I'd have to check with my team but, uh, off the top of my head I'm thinking maybe 150 to 200 accounts, something in that range. We were testing SSO integrations with a few internal apps.

Okay, so that does line up with the discrepancy — 1,200 contracted plus roughly 200 test accounts would get you to the 1,400 that was billed. The system doesn't differentiate between test and production users unless they're tagged and excluded explicitly, which is something we'd typically walk customers through during a migration engagement.

Right, well, that's — I mean, that's a process thing we probably should have caught on our end too, so I'm not going to sit here and say it's entirely on Aegis. But the question now is what do we do about the invoice.

Absolutely, and that's a reasonable question. So what I can do from a support side is document the root cause here — essentially the overage was driven by non-production test accounts that weren't deprovisioned post-migration — and submit a billing adjustment request to our finance team. That goes to them with my notes and they review it.
Okay. And what's the typical turnaround on something like that?

Usually 5 to 7 business days for the review, and then if a credit or adjustment is approved it would appear on your next billing cycle. I can't guarantee the outcome of the review, but given that the overage appears to be tied to test accounts from a migration, it's a scenario our billing team has handled before.
Cool, thanks.

That's fine. I'd appreciate you submitting that. And — sorry, one more thing — those test accounts, I need to get those cleaned up regardless. Is there a way to bulk deprovision them, or does someone have to go in and do it one by one?

Yeah, there's a bulk deprovision option in the Aegis Identity admin console — if you go to User Management and then Provisioning, you can filter by account status, creation date, and there's also a tag-based filter if any of those test accounts were tagged during setup. You can select multiple accounts and deprovision them in one action.
Okay good, that's helpful. I'll have my team take care of that this week. We definitely don't want to have the same issue on the February invoice.

That makes sense, and I'd actually recommend doing it sooner rather than later so that the February billing period doesn't pick up the elevated seat count again. If you want, I can send you a quick knowledge base article that walks through the bulk deprovision steps — sometimes it's easier to have that written out.
Yeah, that would be useful, if you could send that to the email on the case that'd be great.
Will do. I'll send that along with a summary of what we discussed today and the billing adjustment request reference number once I've submitted it. Should all come through within the next hour or so.

Perfect. And just — out of curiosity — going forward, is there a way to get some kind of alert or notification if our seat count gets close to the contracted limit? Because I'd rather know proactively than find out on an invoice.

That's actually a really good question. There are usage threshold alerts you can configure in the admin console — under Account Settings, Billing and Usage, you can set a threshold, say 90% of your contracted seat count, and you'll get an email notification when you hit it. That's probably worth setting up.
Yeah, I'll definitely look into that. I don't think we have that configured right now.
I'll include a note about that in the follow-up email as well, just so you have the steps. Is there anything else I can help you with on this call today?
No, I think that covers it. I appreciate you walking through all of this — it's helpful to understand what actually happened rather than just having a number on a bill that doesn't make sense.

Of course, that's what we're here for. I'll get everything documented and submitted, and you should hear back from the billing team within that 5 to 7 business day window. If you have any other questions in the meantime, feel free to reply to the case email and it'll come back to me.
Sounds good. Thanks Sarah.
Thank you Gregory, have a good rest of your day.
Alright, I think we've got everyone on now — Tom, you there?
Yeah, I'm here, sorry — had to jump off another call, go ahead.
No worries. Okay, so usual format — let's do a quick status round, then I want to spend some time on the Detect pipeline work and the Comply v2 timeline. Mike, you want to kick us off?

Sure. So on my end, I've been heads down on the event ingestion refactor. We're about sixty percent through the work — the new queue partitioning is done, and I've got a draft PR up for the retry logic. Still need to wire up the dead letter queue handling and then we can start integration testing.
And that's all under the Detect reliability track, right?
Yeah, exactly. It's all scoped to the pipeline work.
Good. Tyler, how about you?

Um, so I finished up the alert deduplication changes last week — those are merged. Right now I'm picking up the monitoring dashboard tickets that got bumped from last sprint. There's like three or four of them, they're not huge but they've been sitting there for a while.
Yeah those have been in the backlog forever, good to finally get to them. Any blockers?

Not right now. I might need some time with Mike later this week to ask a few questions about how the ingestion layer works because some of the dashboard stuff pulls from there, but nothing blocking me today.
Yeah, loud and clear.
Okay, Tom, anything from your side before we get into the weeds on the pipeline stuff?

Yeah, a couple things. One — I had a conversation with product Friday and they're asking again about the timeline for the Detect reliability work. They want to know if we're still comfortable with end of March. And two — the Comply v2 launch is tracking for early April, so I want to make sure we haven't over-allocated anyone against Detect to where we can't support any last-minute Comply stuff.
Right, yeah — on the end of March question, I think we're okay but it's tight. Mike, do you want to give your honest read on that?

Honestly? End of March for the core pipeline changes is doable, but that's assuming we don't hit anything weird in integration testing. If the testing surfaces issues — which it might — we're looking at a week or two of buffer that we don't really have. So I'd call it like... sixty-forty we hit the date.
Okay so not super confident. That's useful to know now rather than in three weeks.
Yeah, I'd rather flag it now. I don't want to sandbag the estimate but I also don't want to commit to something and then miss it.
I get it. What would it take to move the odds more in our favor? Like is this a resourcing thing or just inherent uncertainty in the work?

Bit of both, honestly. The dead letter queue piece and the circuit breaker pattern — those are well-understood, I'm not worried about them. The part I'm less sure about is how the redundant node setup interacts with our current Kafka config. We haven't fully mapped that out yet.
Could Tyler help spike on the Kafka piece? Is that something he could pick up, or is it too deep in your area?
I mean, I could take a look at it. I'm not as familiar with our Kafka setup as Mike is, but I've worked with Kafka before. It might take me a bit to get context but I'm not opposed to it.

The risk there is the ramp-up time kind of eats into whatever time savings you'd get. But — if Tyler can do some of the investigation work and document what he finds, that could at least give me clearer inputs so I'm not going in blind. That might actually help.
Okay let's do that. Tyler, can you carve out time this week to start pulling on that thread? Just document what you find, even if it's rough notes — Mike, you can work off that.
Yeah, works for me.
Good. Tom, does that give you enough to go back to product with, or do they need a harder commitment?

I'll tell them end of March is still the target but flag that there's some technical uncertainty we're actively working down. I'd rather do that than give them a false sense of confidence. They won't love it but they'll deal with it.

Yeah, that's the right call. Okay — on the Comply v2 resource question, I don't think we have a huge exposure there. The team that's been carrying that work is mostly on the platform side. The main thing I'd watch is if QA needs help in late March, early April — that's where it could bleed over.
Who's the primary contact on that side, is it still Priya's team?

Yeah, Priya owns it. I've been syncing with her weekly. She hasn't raised any red flags yet — the on-demand reporting piece is apparently in pretty good shape, and the multi-framework stuff for SOC 2, PCI, HIPAA is mostly done. The ISO 27001 mapping was the last thing to come in.
Okay good. Keep that sync going, and just make sure she knows to flag us early if she needs anything from this team.

Will do. Alright, anything else on Comply before we move on? ... No? Okay. Let me just do a quick check on the sprint board — we've got eight tickets in progress, two in review. The two in review are Tyler's dedup changes — wait, those are merged already.
Yeah they should show as done, I closed them out Friday.

Board probably just hasn't updated. I'll fix it after this call. Okay so eight in progress, nothing blocking at the moment. Mike, when do you think the PR will be ready for review — the retry logic one?
Probably Wednesday. It's in draft right now, I want to clean it up a bit before I move it to ready. There's some stuff in there I'm not fully happy with yet.
That's fine. I'll plan to take a look Thursday then. Alright, I think that covers everything — Tom, anything before we close out?
No, I'm good. Good check-in, appreciate the transparency on the timeline stuff. Keep me posted if anything changes.
Will do. Alright everyone, have a good week.
Thanks, you too.
Yeah, take it easy.
Hey Thomas, can you hear us okay?
Yeah, loud and clear — good to hear from you guys, it's been a couple weeks.
Good, good — yeah sorry it took us a bit to get this on the calendar, things have been moving fast on our end with the Comply v2 launch and everything.
Actually that's kind of why we're here today, right, so — Thomas, I also want to introduce Kevin, he's our solutions architect and he's going to be the technical lead on your ISO 27001 prep work.
Hey Thomas, great to meet you — I've been looking forward to this one, Daniel's told me a lot about what Redwood is trying to accomplish this year.

Likewise, yeah, and honestly the timing could not be better because our CISO has been breathing down my neck about ISO 27001 since like January, so — any help getting a clearer picture of where we stand is going to be huge.

Yeah, absolutely, and I think that's exactly where Comply v2 is going to change the game for you — um, before we dive in though, Thomas, can you just give Kevin a quick sense of where you are in the ISO journey? Like, have you done a formal gap assessment yet or are you still kind of in the early stages?

Yeah so we're — we're pretty early if I'm being honest. We had an external consultant come in back in February, they did a high-level gap assessment, and the short version is we have a lot of work to do on the information security management side, particularly around evidence collection and demonstrating continuous monitoring, which as a healthcare organization is like, that's a big deal for us.

Right, and that's such a common pain point, especially in healthcare — the auditors want to see a continuous thread of evidence, not just a snapshot, and that's historically been really painful to pull together manually.
Oh god, yeah, the last time we went through a HIPAA audit my team spent like three weeks just pulling logs and documentation together — it was a nightmare.
Three weeks, yeah — that's — I mean that's not unusual but it's also exactly the kind of thing we built Comply v2 to eliminate, so let's talk about what that actually looks like in practice.
Please, yeah, walk me through it.

So — okay so the big thing with v2, and this just went GA on April 7th so it's very fresh, is the on-demand reporting engine. You can now generate a full ISO 27001 readiness report at any point, and it maps your current control posture directly to the Annex A controls — so you're not having to manually cross-reference anything, the platform does that work for you.
Wait, so it's actually mapping to the specific Annex A controls? Like, is that — is it just a template thing or is it actually pulling live data from our environment?

Live data — so it's pulling from your Detect telemetry, your Identity configurations, your Protect backup logs, and then surfacing all of that against the control framework. So when an auditor asks you to demonstrate, say, A.12.4 on event logging and monitoring, you can literally generate that section of the report on the spot.
Okay that is — yeah that is genuinely impressive, I wasn't expecting that level of granularity.

And it's not just ISO either — you mentioned HIPAA a second ago — the same report engine covers HIPAA, SOC 2, and PCI DSS as well, so if you ever need to spin up a HIPAA evidence package, it's the same workflow, same interface.
Yeah that multi-framework piece is something we specifically asked for in our renewal discussions, so it's great to see that actually shipped — Daniel you might remember that conversation.
I do, yeah, I have the notes from that call — it was definitely on the roadmap and I'm glad we could deliver on it before your audit cycle.

So Thomas, one thing I want to make sure we cover — in terms of your timeline, do you have a target date for the ISO 27001 certification audit? Because that's going to shape how we structure the onboarding to Comply v2.
Our CISO is targeting Q1 2027 for the formal audit, so we have — you know, roughly eight or nine months, which sounds like a lot but when you factor in the remediation work it's going to go fast.

Eight months is actually a really solid runway for this — um, what I'd typically recommend for organizations at your stage is we start with a baseline report run in the first two weeks, just to get a concrete picture of where you are against the framework, and then we build a remediation roadmap from those gaps.
Yeah and that baseline report — is that something Kevin can help us interpret, or is it pretty self-explanatory on the platform?

Both, honestly — the report is designed to be readable by a non-specialist, we have like a traffic light system for control status, but Kevin and I will absolutely walk through it with you line by line on a follow-up call.

Yeah I was going to say — I'd actually love to do a working session with you and maybe whoever on your team is going to be the compliance lead, we can go through the report together and I can explain what each gap actually means in practical terms, not just the framework language.
That would be — yeah that would be incredibly helpful, I have a security analyst, her name is Priya, she's been leading the internal ISO prep work and she would get a lot out of that kind of session.

Perfect, let's make sure Priya is on the next call — I'll send a calendar invite for a technical working session, maybe two weeks out, that gives you time to get Comply v2 activated and run that first report.
That works — and just so I understand the activation piece, is that something I need to loop in my IT team for, or is it more of a — like a configuration thing on the Aegis side?

It's pretty lightweight on your end — since you're already running Detect and Identity, the data connectors are mostly already in place, we just need to enable the Comply v2 module on your tenant and do a quick configuration review to make sure all your data sources are mapped correctly, should take maybe an hour of your team's time.
Oh wow, okay, yeah that's much less overhead than I was anticipating — I was kind of bracing myself for like a big implementation project.
Yeah that's the benefit of being on the full platform — you're not starting from scratch, we're just unlocking capabilities that the underlying data already supports.

You know, I'll be honest — when we were evaluating platforms last year, one of the things that tipped us toward Aegis was exactly that, the idea that everything would be integrated rather than bolted together, and it's good to see that actually playing out in practice.

That means a lot to hear, Thomas — and we want to make sure it keeps playing out that way through the audit, so Kevin's going to be your dedicated point of contact for all things Comply through this ISO process.
Yeah, consider me on speed dial — seriously, if you or Priya hit questions on the platform or you're not sure how to interpret something in the report, just reach out, that's what I'm here for.

Really appreciate that — okay so just to make sure I've got the next steps straight: Kevin sends a calendar invite for the technical working session in two weeks, Daniel gets Comply v2 activated on our tenant, and in the meantime we — or I guess Kevin's team handles the module enablement, and then we run the first baseline report before that working session.
That's exactly it — I'll also send you a short prep document that explains what to expect from the baseline report so you and Priya aren't going in cold.
Perfect — honestly this call was really reassuring, I feel like we actually have a path forward now, which is not how I felt last week.
That's exactly what we want to hear — alright Thomas, we'll let you get back to it, talk soon.
Sounds great, thanks guys — really looking forward to it.
Hey Lauren, thanks for making time this morning — I know Q2 planning season gets pretty hectic.
Yeah, no kidding, it's been a lot. But this was already on my calendar so I made sure to protect it.

I appreciate that. So, just to set the agenda for today — I wanted to walk through where things stand on the renewal, get your feedback on how the platform's been performing, and then we can talk about what your roadmap looks like for the rest of the year.
That works. And I do have some things I want to flag, so... yeah, let's just get into it.
Of course. Why don't you go first actually — what's on your mind?
Okay, so the big thing — and I want to be direct here — is the outage in March. The Detect outage. That was... that was not a great experience for us.
Yeah, and I completely understand. I was going to bring that up myself. That was March 10th through roughly the 18th, the event processing issue — six hours of no visibility on threat monitoring.
Right. And look, I get that outages happen, I've been in infrastructure long enough to know nothing is bulletproof, but six hours is a long time. Our security team was basically flying blind.
Completely fair. And I don't want to just gloss over it with a, you know, 'here's what we did to fix it' kind of answer if you have more to say about the impact on your side.

I mean, we didn't have an incident during that window, which is lucky, but it surfaced a gap in our own contingency planning too, so it wasn't all on Aegis. It just... it raised questions internally about the reliability of the platform.

That's really helpful context, and I appreciate you being candid about it. On our end, the root cause was a single point of failure in the event ingestion pipeline — it was a cascading failure from there. Engineering has since deployed redundant processing nodes and implemented a circuit breaker pattern, so the architecture is fundamentally different now in that layer.
Okay. Is there documentation on that? Like, something I can actually share with my team rather than just taking your word for it?
Yes, absolutely. We put out a full post-incident review — I'll send that over after the call. It goes into the root cause analysis, the remediation steps, and the testing we did before re-deploying.
Good. That'll help. My team wants to see that it's not just a band-aid.
Understood. And honestly if there are follow-up questions after your team reviews it, I can also set up time with one of our solution architects to walk through the technical details.
That might be worth doing, yeah. Let me see what the team thinks after they read the doc. Okay, so that's the main concern. What else — you said you wanted to cover the renewal specifically?
Right. So your current contract runs through end of June, so we're about... eleven weeks out. I wanted to start the conversation early so we're not scrambling in June.
Smart. Yeah. What are you looking at — is pricing changing?

So, there is a standard escalator built into the contract — I believe it's three percent — and that would apply. But I also want to understand what your scope looks like going into next year before we lock anything in, because if you're expanding, that changes the conversation.
Mm. Yeah, so we're planning to add about forty more cloud workloads through Q3 as we finish migrating the last of our on-prem stuff. So the footprint is growing.
Okay, that's good to know. Are those workloads going to be on CloudPrime primarily, or is it a mix?
Mix. Mostly CloudPrime, some Azure — we've got about a 70/30 split right now and I think it stays roughly there.

Got it. That's helpful. So I'll put together some options — probably a base renewal at your current tier, and then a growth option that prices in that expanded workload count. I can model out what makes sense financially for both sides.
That'd be useful. And, um, I do want to flag — we're not exclusively evaluating Aegis. I have procurement pushing me to get at least one competitive quote, so... I want to be transparent about that.
I appreciate you being upfront. Who are you looking at, if you don't mind me asking?
Yes, hi David, thanks for calling back. I've been waiting on this — we submitted that ticket two days ago.
Probably SentinelShield, just because they've been pretty aggressive in our space lately. I don't have a strong pull toward them but procurement wants the comparison.
Fair enough. I'll make sure our proposal is competitive on value, not just price. And I'm happy to do a side-by-side if that would be helpful for your internal review.
Maybe. Let me see what SentinelShield comes back with first.
Of course. Just know the offer is there. Okay, I also wanted to touch on Comply v2 — you're currently on Comply, right? The new version just went GA on April 7th.
Oh yeah, I saw the release notes. The on-demand reporting — we've actually already poked around in it a little.
Oh great, any early impressions?

It's... decent. The SOC 2 report generation is noticeably faster than v1. We haven't dug into the HIPAA or ISO stuff yet because that's not really our priority, but the multi-framework view is something we'll probably use for PCI.

That's good to hear on the SOC 2 side — that's been one of the most common things customers come to us about. And yeah, PCI DSS coverage in v2 is pretty robust, so I'd encourage you to test that whenever you get a chance.
We will. Our next PCI audit cycle is Q4 so I want to have that dialed in before then.
Perfect timing then. I can connect you with our compliance specialist if you want a guided walkthrough before you're in the thick of audit prep.
Yeah, actually, that would be useful. Maybe in June or so.

I'll make a note of that.
